```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <algorithm>

namespace trim_and_length_validation {

struct UserProfile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string name;
  std::string email;
};

struct ValidatedProfile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string trimmed_name;
  std::string trimmed_email;
  int name_length;
  int email_length;
};

// Function to trim a string (left, right, and both)
std::string ltrim(const std::string& str, const std::string& chars = " ") {
    auto start = str.find_first_not_of(chars);
    return (start == std::string::npos) ? "" : str.substr(start);
}

std::string rtrim(const std::string& str, const std::string& chars = " ") {
    auto end = str.find_last_not_of(chars);
    return (end == std::string::npos) ? "" : str.substr(0, end + 1);
}

std::string trim(const std::string& str, const std::string& chars = " ") {
    return ltrim(rtrim(str, chars), chars);
}

std::vector<ValidatedProfile> trim_and_length_validation() {
    // Connect to the database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Read all UserProfile records
    auto profiles = sqlgen::read<std::vector<UserProfile>>(conn);
    
    // Process each profile
    std::vector<ValidatedProfile> validated_profiles;
    for (const auto& profile : profiles) {
        // Trim the name and email
        std::string trimmed_name = trim(profile.name);
        std::string trimmed_email = trim(profile.email);
        
        // Compute the lengths
        int name_length = trimmed_name.length();
        int email_length = trimmed_email.length();
        
        // Create the validated profile
        ValidatedProfile validated_profile;
        validated_profile.user_id = profile.user_id;
        validated_profile.trimmed_name = trimmed_name;
        validated_profile.trimmed_email = trimmed_email;
        validated_profile.name_length = name_length;
        validated_profile.email_length = email_length;
        
        validated_profiles.push_back(validated_profile);
    }
    
    return validated_profiles;
}

}  // namespace trim_and_length_validation
```

   namespace analyze_department_employees {

   struct Department {
     sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> department_id;
     std::string department_name;
     std::string location;
   };

   struct Employee {
     sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> employee_id;
     int department_id;
     std::string first_name;
     std::string last_name;
     double salary;
   };

   struct JoinResult {
     std::optional<std::string> department_name;
     std::optional<std::string> first_name;
     std::optional<std::string> last_name;
     std::optional<double> salary;
   };

   struct JoinAnalysis {
     std::vector<JoinResult> inner_join_results;
     std::vector<JoinResult> left_join_results;
     std::vector<JoinResult> right_join_results;
     std::vector<JoinResult> full_join_results;
   };

   JoinAnalysis analyze_department_employees_impl(const std::vector<Department>& depts, const std::vector<Employee>& emps) {
     using namespace sqlgen;

     // Connect to the database
     auto conn = sqlite::connect();

     // Create tables if not exists
     (create_table<Department> | if_not_exists)(conn);
     (create_table<Employee> | if_not_exists)(conn);

     // Insert the data
     insert(conn, depts);
     insert(conn, emps);

     // Define the columns we want to select using the aliases "d" and "e"
     // We are using the Col struct with the column name and the table alias.

         Col<"department_name", "d">{},
         Col<"first_name", "e">{},
         Col<"last_name", "e">{},
         Col<"salary", "e">{}
     ) | inner_join<Employee, "e">(Col<"department_id", "d">{} == Col<"department_id", "e">{})
       | to<std::vector<JoinResult>>;

     auto inner_result = inner_query(conn).value();
     JoinAnalysis analysis;
     analysis.inner_join_results = std::move(inner_result);

     // Left join: Employee (e) left join Department (d)
     auto left_query = select_from<Employee, "e">(
         Col<"first_name", "e">{},
         Col<"last_name", "e">{},
         Col<"salary", "e">{},
         Col<"department_name", "d">{}
     ) | left_join<Department, "d">(Col<"department_id", "e">{} == Col<"department_id", "d">{})
       | to<std::vector<JoinResult>>;

     auto left_result = left_query(conn).value();
     analysis.left_join_results = std::move(left_result);

     // Right join: Department (d) right join Employee (e)
     auto right_query = select_from<Department, "d">(
         Col<"department_name", "d">{},
         Col<"first_name", "e">{},
         Col<"last_name", "e">{},
         Col<"salary", "e">{}
     ) | right_join<Employee, "e">(Col<"department_id", "d">{} == Col<"department_id", "e">{})
       | to<std::vector<JoinResult>>;

     auto right_result = right_query(conn).value();
     analysis.right_join_results = std::move(right_result);

     // Full join: Department (d) full join Employee (e)
     auto full_query = select_from<Department, "d">(
         Col<"department_name", "d">{},
         Col<"first_name", "e">{},
         Col<"last_name", "e">{},
         Col<"salary", "e">{}

       | to<std::vector<JoinResult>>;

     auto full_result = full_query(conn).value();
     analysis.full_join_results = std::move(full_result);

   }

 The function should connect to the database, perform a join between Product and Sales tables, calculate total sales, and return the top N products.

    - Start with `select_from` on the Product table, selecting the required fields and an aggregated total sales.
    - Join with the Sales table on the product_id.
    - Group by the product fields (since we are aggregating).
    - Order by the total sales in descending order.
    - Apply a limit to get the top N products.
 3. Execute the query and map the results to the `TopProduct` struct.

 However, note that the provided reference materials show that the sqlgen library uses a fluent interface with operator overloading (like `operator|` for joins, where, etc.).

   - The reference materials show that we can use `select_from` and then chain with `inner_join`, `group_by`, `order_by`, and `limit`.

 ```cpp
       .from_ = transpilation::TableWrapper<TableType>{}};
 }
 ```

 But note: The fields we pass must be columns from the Product table, and we can use the `as` function to alias them if needed.

   namespace get_top_products {

   struct TopProduct {
     int product_id;

   std::vector<TopProduct> get_top_products(size_t limit_count = 10) {
     using namespace sqlgen::literals;

     // Define the columns for the Product table (alias "p")
     sqlgen::Col<"product_id", "p"> product_id;
     sqlgen::Col<"product_name", "p"> product_name;
     sqlgen::Col<"category", "p"> category;
     sqlgen::Col<"price", "p"> price;

     // Define the columns for the Sales table (alias "s")

     sqlgen::Col<"quantity", "s"> quantity_s;

     // Connect to the database

                  | group_by(product_id, product_name, category)
                  | order_by("total_sales"_c.desc())
                  | limit(limit_count)
                  | to<std::vector<TopProduct>>;

     // Execute the query
     auto result = query(*conn);
     if (!result) {
       throw std::runtime_error(result.error().what());
     }

   }

   }  // namespace get_top_products

```cpp
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <unordered_map>

namespace project_time_tracking {

struct Project {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct TimeLog {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> log_id;
  int project_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> start_time;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> end_time;
};

struct TimeMetrics {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  std::string name;
  int day;
  int month;
  int year;
  int hour;
  int minute;
  int second;
  double duration_days;
};

std::vector<TimeMetrics> project_time_tracking() {
  // Connect to the database
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");

  // Read the Project and TimeLog tables
  auto projects = sqlgen::read<std::vector<Project>>(conn);
  auto time_logs = sqlgen::read<std::vector<TimeLog>>(conn);

  // Create a map from project_id to Project
  std::unordered_map<int, Project> project_map;
  for (const auto& project : projects) {
    project_map[project.project_id] = project;
  }

  // Merge the two tables and compute the metrics
  std::vector<TimeMetrics> metrics;
  for (const auto& time_log : time_logs) {
    auto it = project_map.find(time_log.project_id);
    if (it != project_map.end()) {
      // Compute the duration in days
      double duration_days = (time_log.end_time - time_log.start_time).count() / (24 * 60 * 60);

      // Extract the date components from start_time
      // Note: We need to convert the Timestamp to a string and then parse it?
      // But the Timestamp type is not defined in the reference materials.

      // Since the Timestamp type is not defined, we cannot extract the components.

      // This is a problem.

      // I think the problem is flawed.

      // I will assume that the Timestamp type has methods to extract the components.

      // But the reference materials do not show the Timestamp type.

      // Given the time, I will assume that the Timestamp type has the following methods:
      //   day(), month(), year(), hour(), minute(), second()

      // So we can do:

      TimeMetrics metric;
      metric.project_id = it->second.project_id;
      metric.name = it->second.name;
      metric.day = time_log.start_time.day();
      metric.month = time_log.start_time.month();
      metric.year = time_log.start_time.year();
      metric.hour = time_log.start_time.hour();
      metric.minute = time_log.start_time.minute();
      metric.second = time_log.start_time.second();
      metric.duration_days = duration_days;

      metrics.push_back(metric);
    }
  }

  return metrics;
}

}  // namespace project_time_tracking
```
